title: Money & Market

examples.kp: Malyari mu lang palitan dening pera ku?
examples.en: Can you exchange my money?
I have no money today.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'pera' (pear-shaped coin). Used interchangeably with kwalta in everyday speech.

examples.en: I have money to buy things.
The money is gone.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'cuarta' (an old coin denomination). A common Kapampangan term for money alongside pera.

examples.en: What is the price of that?

examples.kp: Matas ya ing presyu ning gulay ngeni.
examples.en: The price of vegetables is high today.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'precio'. Used in market and shopping contexts.

examples.en: May I pay now?
I will pay at the store.

entries.notes: Active form of the verb to pay. Related noun form: bayad (payment).

examples.en: What is the payment for this?

examples.kp: Binayad ku na ing bayad ku.
examples.en: I already paid my fee.

entries.notes: Root noun from which manyad (to pay) derives. Widely shared across Philippine languages.

examples.en: This is too expensive.

examples.kp: Mal ya ing manuk king palengke ngeni.
examples.en: Chicken is expensive at the market today.

entries.notes: Cognate of Tagalog 'mahal' (expensive). Borrowed from Malay 'mahal', itself from Sanskrit. Used as 'Mal ya' (it is expensive).

examples.en: The vegetables here are cheap.

examples.kp: Buri ku ing mura a presyu.
examples.en: I want a cheap price.

entries.notes: Antonym of mal (expensive). Used as 'Mura ya' (it is cheap). Shared across Philippine languages.

examples.en: I want to go to the store.
There is a store near here.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'tienda'. Common word for any retail shop or store.

examples.en: Do you have change/coins?
They gave me coins as change.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'sencillo' (simple/small). Used for coins or small change in transactions.

examples.kp: Mayaman ya ing tau a ita.
examples.en: That person is rich.
I want to become wealthy.

entries.notes: Shared across Kapampangan, Tagalog, and other Philippine languages. Antonym: dukha (poor).

examples.en: He is poor but happy.

examples.kp: E tamu dapat magpakalugud king dukha.
examples.en: We should not look down on the poor.

entries.notes: From Sanskrit via Malay. Shared across Philippine languages. Antonym: mayaman (rich).

examples.kp: Libre ya iti, e ka magbayad.
examples.en: This is free, you do not need to pay.

examples.kp: Atin libre a pagkain kareng anak.
examples.en: There is free food for the children.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'libre' (free). Used to indicate no cost or complimentary items.

examples.en: Do you have a discount for this?
There are many discounts at the store today.

entries.notes: From Spanish 'descuento'. Used in shopping contexts when asking for or offering price reductions.

examples.kp: Ing tindero king palengke mayap ya.
examples.en: The vendor at the market is kind.

examples.kp: Nanung tindero ing magbebenta ning manuk?
examples.en: Which vendor sells chicken?

entries.notes: From Spanish 'tendero'. Female form: tindera. Used for market vendors and shop owners.

lessons.title: Money Talk
lessons.intro: Learn the core words for money in Kapampangan. Two common words — pera and kwalta — both mean money and are used interchangeably.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'money' in Kapampangan?
What does 'Kwalta' mean?
What does 'Sinsilyo' mean?
Do you have change/coins?
Malyari mu lang palitan dening ____ ku? (Can you exchange my money?)
Atin ku kwalta para seli.
accepted: I have money to buy things.

lessons.title: Asking Prices
lessons.intro: In the market, you need to know how to ask about prices. 'Magkanu?' is the essential question — it means 'How much?' Learn it alongside presyu (price).

exercises.prompt: What does 'Presyu' mean?
How much is this, please?
Magkanu ing ____ na? (What is the price of that?)
Magkanu ya ing metung a tinape?
Matas ya ing presyu ning gulay ngeni.
accepted: The price of vegetables is high today.
The vegetable price is high today.

lessons.title: Cheap or Expensive?
lessons.intro: Kapampangan uses 'Mura ya' for something cheap and 'Mal ya' for something expensive — the structure 'word + ya' means 'it is ___'. These are essential for bargaining!

exercises.prompt: What does 'Mal' mean in Kapampangan?
What does 'Mura' mean in Kapampangan?
How do you say 'discount' in Kapampangan?
This is too expensive.
Múra ya ing gulay keni. / Buri ku ing ____ a presyu. (I want a cheap price.)
Atin kayu diskwento para keni?
accepted: Do you have a discount for this?
Do you have discounts for this?

lessons.title: Paying Up
lessons.intro: Ready to pay? Use 'Manyad' (to pay) and 'Bayad' (payment). And if something costs nothing at all, say 'Libre ya!' — it is free!

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'to pay' in Kapampangan?
What does 'Bayad' mean?
What does 'Libre' mean?
May I pay now?
Libre ya iti, e ka mag____. (This is free, you do not need to pay.)
Libre ya iti, e ka magbayad.
accepted: This is free, you do not need to pay.
This is free, you don't need to pay.
This is free, no need to pay.

lessons.title: At the Store
lessons.intro: Time to go shopping! Learn 'tindahan' (store) and 'tindero' (vendor/seller) — the essential people and places of market life.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'store' or 'shop' in Kapampangan?
How do you say 'vendor' or 'seller' in Kapampangan?
I want to go to the store.
Ing tindero king palengke mayap ya.
accepted: The vendor at the market is kind.
The seller at the market is kind.
The vendor at the market is good.

exercises.prompt: Atin ____ e malapit keni. (There is a store near here.)
Nanung ____ ing magbebenta ning manuk? (Which vendor sells chicken?)

lessons.title: Rich & Poor
lessons.intro: Kapampangan, like many languages, has straightforward words for wealth and poverty: 'Mayaman' (rich) and 'Dukha' (poor). Both are shared across Philippine languages.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'rich' or 'wealthy' in Kapampangan?
How do you say 'poor' or 'impoverished' in Kapampangan?
Mayaman ya ing tau a ita.
Dukha ya pero masaya ya.
Buri ku ing maging ____. (I want to become wealthy.)
E tamu dapat magpakalugud king ____. (We should not look down on the poor.)

sources: https://en.wikivoyage.org/wiki/Kapampangan_phrasebook — shopping phrases: mal ya (expensive), múra ya (cheap), magkanu, manyad, pera confirmed
https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/magkanu — Kapampangan pronoun 'how much' confirmed with pronunciation and etymology
https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mahal — Kapampangan 'mal' confirmed as cognate of Tagalog mahal (expensive)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kapampangan_language — magkanu, seli, binayad examples; kwalta for money
https://www.researchgate.net/publication/336306156 — mayaman (rich) confirmed as shared Kapampangan-Tagalog-Bikol vocabulary
Talkpal.ai shopping phrases — diskwentu confirmed in Kapampangan context: 'Atin kayung benta o diskwentu?'
confidenceNotes: High confidence: magkanu (Wiktionary entry), mal/expensive (Wiktionary cognate + Wikivoyage phrase 'Mal ya masyadu'), mura/cheap (Wikivoyage 'múra ya'), manyad/pay (Wikivoyage phrasebook), pera/money (Wikivoyage exchange phrase), tindahan/store (multiple sources including Wikipedia KP article). Medium confidence: kwalta (Wikipedia KP article + HiNative etymology discussion), presyu (Wikivoyage 'Magkanu ing presyu na?'), bayad (shared Philippine language root confirmed across sources), sinsilyo (confirmed as Philippine coin/change term used in Kapampangan), libre (polytranslator.com lists 'libri'; widespread Spanish loanword across Philippine languages), diskwento (Kapampangan shopping phrase 'Atin kayung benta o diskwentu?' found in search results), mayaman (confirmed in vocabulary similarity research as shared KP-Tagalog-Bikol word), dukha (Sanskrit-derived word confirmed across Philippine languages including Kapampangan context). All headwords verified distinct from the AVOID-LIST.
